walkthrough.title: Meet the Cards
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Apples to Apples, the party game where the funniest answer wins. There are two types of cards in the box: green apple cards have adjectives like "Scary" or "Delicious," and red apple cards have nouns like "My Prom Date" or "Darth Vader." Let's get familiar with both.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle Both Decks
walkthrough.text: Let's separate all the green apple cards into one pile and all the red apple cards into another. Shuffle each deck thoroughly and place them face-down in the center of the table. The green deck is the judge's deck, and the red deck is everyone else's ammunition.

walkthrough.title: Deal Red Cards
walkthrough.text: Everyone picks up their hand and looks at their cards without showing anyone else. These seven cards are your options for every round, and you will draw back up to seven after each one.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Judge
walkthrough.text: Let's pick who judges first. The person who most recently ate an apple goes first, or just pick the player whose birthday is coming up next. The judge role rotates to the left after every round, so everyone gets a turn.

walkthrough.title: Know the Win Condition
walkthrough.text: Let's go over how many green cards you need to win. With four players you need eight, five players need seven, six need six, seven need five, and eight or more need only four. Fewer players means a longer game.

summary.title: Meet the Cards
bullets: Green apple cards = adjectives (Scary, Delicious, etc.)
Red apple cards = nouns (My Prom Date, Darth Vader, etc.)

summary.title: Shuffle Both Decks
bullets: Separate greens and reds into 2 decks
Shuffle each, place face-down in center

summary.title: Deal Red Cards
bullets: Deal 7 red cards to each player
Players view their own hand privately

summary.title: Choose the First Judge
bullets: Most recently ate an apple, or pick any method
Judge role rotates left each round

summary.title: Know the Win Condition
bullets: 4 players → 8 green cards to win
5 players → 7, 6 players → 6, 7 players → 5
8+ players → 4 green cards to win

walkthrough.title: The Judge Flips a Green Card
walkthrough.text: Each round starts with the judge drawing the top card from the green apple deck and flipping it face-up for everyone to see. Let's say the green card says "Disgusting." The judge reads it aloud so the whole table knows what adjective they are playing to.

walkthrough.title: Players Submit Red Cards
walkthrough.text: Every player except the judge looks at their hand and picks one red apple card that they think best matches the green card. Let's place our chosen red cards face-down in a pile in front of the judge. You want to pick the card the judge will like most, not the one you think is funniest.

walkthrough.title: The Judge Shuffles and Reads
walkthrough.text: Once all red cards are in, the judge shuffles them so nobody knows who played what. Then the judge flips them over one at a time and reads each one aloud. This is the best part of the game, so let's enjoy the reactions and laughter as each card is revealed.

walkthrough.title: The Judge Picks a Winner
walkthrough.text: The judge picks their favorite red card, and there are no rules about how they decide — funniest, most accurate, most creative, most absurd, anything goes. The player who submitted the winning red card reveals themselves and takes the green card as their point.

walkthrough.title: Draw and Rotate
walkthrough.text: After the winner takes the green card, all players draw red cards from the deck until they have seven in hand again. Let's pass the judge role one seat to the left. That player is now the new judge and draws the next green card.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: Let's keep playing rounds until someone collects enough green cards to win. Remember, the number depends on player count: eight for four players, down to four for eight or more. The first player to reach the target announces their win and the game is over.

walkthrough.title: What the Judge Cannot Do
walkthrough.text: Let's be clear about one important rule: the judge does not play a red card during their turn. They only flip the green card, read the submissions, and pick a winner. The judge is neutral for that round and cannot win their own green card.

summary.title: The Judge Flips a Green Card
bullets: Judge draws top green card, reads adjective aloud
All players see the adjective they must match

summary.title: Players Submit Red Cards
bullets: Each non-judge player picks 1 red card from hand
Submit face-down to the judge

summary.title: The Judge Shuffles and Reads
bullets: Judge shuffles submissions → anonymous
Reads each red card aloud

summary.title: The Judge Picks a Winner
bullets: Judge picks favorite red card — any criteria allowed
Winning player takes the green card as 1 point

summary.title: Draw and Rotate
bullets: All players draw back up to 7 red cards
Judge role passes 1 seat to the left

summary.title: Winning the Game
bullets: First to reach green card target wins
4p=8, 5p=7, 6p=6, 7p=5, 8+=4 green cards

summary.title: What the Judge Cannot Do
bullets: Judge does NOT play a red card on their turn
Judge only flips green, reads submissions, picks winner

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Party
walkthrough.text: We are going to walk through a few rounds with seven players to see how the game flows. Seven is the sweet spot because there are six submissions per round, and you should follow along even if your group is a different size.

walkthrough.title: Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Dana reads people well, Marcus is a confident jokester, and Priya is our thoughtful newcomer who is about to learn some lessons the hard way. Eli loves absurd humor, Rosa is a strategic veteran, Jin keeps it simple, and Tasha lobbies shamelessly.

walkthrough.title: Round One: The Green Card
walkthrough.text: Marcus is our first judge and flips the green card: "Scary." Dana remembers Marcus is a huge horror movie fan who hates clowns, so she plays "Clowns." Priya plays "Tax Season" because she genuinely finds taxes terrifying, and the other four players submit their cards face-down too.

walkthrough.title: Marcus Picks a Winner
walkthrough.text: Marcus shuffles all six submissions and reads them aloud one by one. He immediately gravitates toward "Clowns" because he genuinely finds them terrifying, while "Tax Season" gets a polite chuckle but does not resonate. Marcus picks "Clowns" as his winner, and Dana takes the green card.

walkthrough.title: Mistake One: Playing to Yourself Instead of the Judge
walkthrough.text: Priya is confused because Tax Season is objectively scary, but Marcus is a twenty-two-year-old who has never filed taxes. "Tax Season" means nothing to him. Dana won because she played to Marcus's taste, not her own.

walkthrough.title: Lesson One: Read the Judge
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in Apples to Apples is reading the judge. Every judge has different tastes, different humor, and different associations. Before you pick your red card, ask yourself what this specific person would find funny or fitting, and forget what you think the best answer is.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Reading the Judge in Action
walkthrough.text: The judge role passes left. Priya judges and flips "Legendary." Rosa knows Priya studies mythology in college, so she plays "Zeus." Eli plays a strong card, "Dinosaurs." Priya reads through all the submissions and picks "Zeus" because Greek mythology is her thing. Rosa wins the green card by paying attention to who Priya is.

walkthrough.title: Round Three: Hoarding a Strong Card
walkthrough.text: Eli judges and flips "Addictive." Priya spots "Chocolate" in her hand — a perfect match — but decides to save it for a green card where it will be even more perfect, and plays "Reality TV" instead. Eli picks Tasha's "Video Games" as the winner, and Priya's weaker card does not even come close.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Two and Lesson Two: Play Your Best Card Now
walkthrough.text: Priya still has "Chocolate" stuck in her hand, waiting for a perfect green card that may never come — she might be the judge when that ideal card appears, or she might draw new cards before she ever uses it. Do not hoard strong cards for a dream scenario. A good card played now beats a great card stuck in your hand forever.

walkthrough.title: Round Four: Lobbying in Action
walkthrough.text: Rosa judges and flips "Cuddly." Everyone submits their cards, and Rosa reads them aloud. When she reads "Puppies," Tasha immediately says, "Oh come on, puppies are the most cuddly thing in the universe!" When Rosa reads "Grandma," Tasha shouts, "Nope, puppies win!" Tasha is openly lobbying the judge, and Rosa is laughing.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Three and Lesson Three: Lobby the Judge
walkthrough.text: Priya submitted "Kittens," a genuinely great answer, but she sat quietly while Tasha campaigned for "Puppies" and Rosa picked "Puppies" because Tasha's enthusiasm made it funnier. Apples to Apples is a social game, not a silent one. Let's react when cards are read, laugh at the right moments, and steer the judge's attention without revealing which card is yours.

walkthrough.title: Priya Puts It All Together
walkthrough.text: A few rounds later, Jin judges and flips "Outrageous." Priya remembers all three lessons. She thinks about Jin specifically and remembers he loves over-the-top action movies. She plays her strongest match right now: "Explosions." When Jin reads the cards aloud, Priya laughs and says, "Whoever played Explosions knows you too well, Jin." Jin picks it and Priya takes the green card.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You saw Dana read the judge, Rosa use personal knowledge, Tasha lobby with pure energy, and Priya learn three crucial lessons: play to the judge's taste, play your best card now, and speak up at the table. You are ready to party.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you jump into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card types, variants, and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Now go make some terrible comparisons!

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Check General Tips for strategy basics
Reference the Appendix for card types and variants
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Know Your Judge
walkthrough.text: The most important skill in Apples to Apples is reading the judge. Before you pick a card, think about who is judging this round — are they literal-minded or do they love absurd humor? Let's pay attention to what kinds of cards each person picks when they judge and use that knowledge in future rounds.

walkthrough.title: Play the Card, Not the Definition
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best play is not the most accurate match — if the green card says "Smooth" and you have "Sandpaper," that might actually win because the irony is hilarious. Let's think about whether the judge prefers literal matches or funny contrasts. Some judges love the obvious answer, and some love the ridiculous one.

walkthrough.title: Dump Your Weak Cards
walkthrough.text: Not every red card in your hand will ever be useful. If you are holding a card that feels impossible to play well, let's use a round with a bad match as a chance to dump it. A new card from the deck has more potential than a dud stuck in your hand.

walkthrough.title: Watch for Judge Patterns
walkthrough.text: Most people are predictable — some judges always pick the funniest card, others always pick the most literal match, and some pick the last card read aloud because it is freshest in their memory. Let's pay attention to these tendencies. After two or three rounds of watching someone judge, you will know exactly what kind of card to play for them.

walkthrough.title: Use Table Talk Wisely
walkthrough.text: Apples to Apples is a social game, so let's laugh when cards are read, make comments, and build energy around the submissions. You can influence the judge by reacting enthusiastically to certain answers without revealing your card. Just be careful not to be so obvious that everyone knows which card is yours.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Overthink It
walkthrough.text: This is a party game, not a strategy game, so let's keep the pace fast and the energy high. If you spend more than fifteen seconds choosing a card, you are thinking too hard. Go with your gut, play the funniest card you see, and keep the game moving.

walkthrough.title: Embrace the Chaos
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the judge picks a card that makes no sense to you, and that is fine. Let's not argue about whether the "right" card won because the judge is always correct by definition. Laugh it off, draw a new card, and get ready for the next round.

walkthrough.title: Adjust to the Group
walkthrough.text: Apples to Apples plays differently with every group — a family game night favors wholesome, clever answers while a group of college friends rewards outrageous picks. Let's pay attention to the room because the same card might win at one table and fall flat at another. Read the group, not just the judge.

summary.title: Know Your Judge
bullets: Think about the judge's humor and personality before picking
Track what types of cards each person picks when judging

summary.title: Play the Card, Not the Definition
bullets: Ironic or absurd matches can beat literal ones
Some judges prefer accuracy, others prefer humor

summary.title: Dump Your Weak Cards
bullets: Use bad-match rounds to discard dead-weight cards
A fresh draw > a dud stuck in hand

summary.title: Watch for Judge Patterns
bullets: Some always pick funniest, some always pick most literal
Some favor the last card read (recency bias)
2-3 rounds of observation reveals tendencies

summary.title: Use Table Talk Wisely
bullets: React to cards, build energy, steer judge attention
Don't be so obvious you reveal your own card

summary.title: Do Not Overthink It
bullets: Spend <15 sec choosing a card
Go with your gut → keeps pace and energy high

summary.title: Embrace the Chaos
bullets: The judge is always "right" by definition
Don't argue results — laugh it off and draw a new card

summary.title: Adjust to the Group
bullets: Family night → wholesome/clever wins
College friends → outrageous/edgy wins
Same card can dominate one table and flop at another

walkthrough.title: Build a Judge Profile
walkthrough.text: Let's take judge-reading to the next level by mentally noting what each judge picks and why after every round. Over a full game, you will build a profile for every player — Marcus always picks funny, Priya picks literal, Rosa picks creative. Use these profiles to tailor your plays precisely.

walkthrough.title: The Decoy Lobby
walkthrough.text: Let's use table talk strategically — when the judge reads your card, stay quiet, and when they read someone else's strong card, loudly agree that it is a great answer. The judge might assume that enthusiastic card is yours, and a competitive judge might avoid picking it. Misdirection is a legitimate party game skill.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Strong Cards
walkthrough.text: While you should not hoard cards forever, let's think one round ahead — if you know who is judging next and you have a card that is perfect for their taste, it is worth holding for one round. The key difference from hoarding is having a specific plan: this card, for this judge, next round. Vague "saving for later" is a mistake, but targeted timing is smart.

walkthrough.title: The Sympathy Play
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about an underrated tactic: if you have been losing for several rounds, some judges will subconsciously root for you. A card that is good but not great might win because the judge feels generous toward the underdog. Do not count on this, but be aware that social dynamics affect judging more than people admit.

walkthrough.title: Control the Pace
walkthrough.text: Fast submissions suggest confidence, and some judges assume the first card in was the most obvious match. You can use or subvert that expectation depending on the judge.

walkthrough.title: Play Across the Spectrum
walkthrough.text: The best players do not always play funny or always play literal. Let's vary our approach based on the judge. Play literal for literal judges, funny for funny judges, and absurd for the wild cards. Consistency makes you predictable, and predictable players get their cards identified and avoided by competitive judges.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Hand
walkthrough.text: If your hand is full of niche cards, dump one per round to cycle through the deck faster. A hand with three to four versatile cards and three specific ones gives you flexibility for any green card. Treat your hand like a portfolio: diversify so you always have something playable.

walkthrough.title: The Endgame Push
walkthrough.text: When you are one green card away from winning, the table dynamic shifts. Let's be aware that other players may gang up to keep you from winning. Judges might avoid your card out of spite. Play your absolute strongest card and do not get cute with irony or long shots. The endgame is about reliability, not creativity.

summary.title: Build a Judge Profile
bullets: Track each player's judging tendencies across the game
Tailor card selection → funny for funny judges, literal for literal

summary.title: The Decoy Lobby
bullets: Stay quiet on your own card, hype a rival's card
Competitive judges may avoid the card they think is "too popular"

summary.title: Timing Your Strong Cards
bullets: Hold a card for 1 round if you know the next judge's taste
Targeted timing ≠ vague hoarding — must have a specific plan

summary.title: The Sympathy Play
bullets: Judges sometimes favor underdogs subconsciously
Social dynamics affect judging more than people admit

summary.title: Control the Pace
bullets: Fast submit = signals confidence/obvious match
Use or subvert judge's pace assumptions

summary.title: Play Across the Spectrum
bullets: Vary between literal, funny, and absurd based on judge
Predictable players get identified and avoided

summary.title: Manage Your Hand
bullets: Dump niche cards to cycle deck faster
Aim for 3-4 versatile cards + 3 specific ones
Diversify so you always have something playable

summary.title: The Endgame Push
bullets: 1 card from winning → table may gang up on you
Play strongest card, avoid risky/ironic picks
Endgame = reliability over creativity

entries.definition: An adjective card flipped by the judge each round. Players compete to match it with their red cards. Winning players collect the green card as a point.
A noun card held in each player's hand. Players submit one red card per round to match the green card. Hands are always refilled to seven.
The player who flips the green card, reads all submissions aloud, and picks the winning red card. The judge does not submit a red card during their turn. Rotates left each round.
The act of placing a red card face-down for the judge. All non-judge players must submit exactly one card per round.
The judge shuffles all submitted red cards, reads them aloud, and picks their favorite. The judge's decision is final and cannot be overruled.
After each round, all players draw red cards from the deck until they hold seven. This happens before the judge role rotates.
Using table talk to influence the judge's decision. Players can react to cards, argue for favorites, or misdirect without revealing which card is theirs.
A variant where one random red card is added from the top of the deck each round, competing alongside player submissions. The mystery card sometimes wins.
A variant where the judge picks the red card that LEAST matches the green card. Rewards deliberately bad or ironic pairings instead of fitting ones.
A variant where each player submits two red cards instead of one. Gives the judge more options and lets players hedge their bets with a safe pick and a wild one.
The number of green cards needed to win, based on player count. Four players need eight, five need seven, six need six, seven need five, eight or more need four.
A player's current set of seven red apple cards. Cards leave your hand when submitted and are replaced by drawing from the red deck.
Deliberately playing a weak or unusable red card during a round you expect to lose, in order to draw a fresh replacement from the deck.
The skill of predicting what kind of red card the current judge will favor, based on their personality, humor, and past judging patterns.
After each round, the judge role passes one seat to the left. Every player takes turns judging, ensuring no one judges two rounds in a row.
